fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the linked data processing library we discussed in last quarter's planning. Basically we want something that can take JSON-LD documents and do all the standard transformations on them — you know, the usual graph stuff. Think of it like that RDF utility we prototyped for the semantic web client last year, but productionized properly.

The big pain point right now is that every team that touches linked data ends up writing their own one-off scripts to handle IRI resolution, graph flattening, context application, etc. and they always end up with subtle data loss or serialization inconsistencies when exchanging data with external RDF systems. We need one canonical place for all of this.

It should handle the full lifecycle: expanding compact documents, squishing them back down with a context, making the graph structure flat and comparable, reshaping data around a template, converting back and forth to the raw triple format, and producing a stable canonical form. There's also some namespace shortening behavior we need — refer to how we handled prefix compaction in the graph client module — and it needs to report problems cleanly without exposing internal exception noise to callers.

The output format needs to be consistent and pretty-printed in a way that downstream tools can parse reliably. Architecture should be clean, multi-file, separated concerns. Don't make it a god file.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: JsonLdProcessor.expand(Object input, JsonLdOptions options) / JsonLdProcessor.compact(Object input, Object context, JsonLdOptions options) / JsonLdProcessor.flatten(Object input, Object context, JsonLdOptions options) / JsonLdProcessor.frame(Object input, Object frame, JsonLdOptions options) / JsonLdProcessor.toRDF(Object input, JsonLdOptions options) / JsonLdProcessor.fromRDF(Object input, JsonLdOptions options) / JsonLdProcessor.normalize(Object input, JsonLdOptions options)

trigger_keywords: what does expanded JSON-LD look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Expanded JSON-LD must be output as a pretty-printed JSON array. Each node object uses full IRIs as keys. Plain string values become `{"@value": "..."}` objects. Typed literals include `@type`. Language-tagged literals include `@language`. Native numbers (integers) are preserved as numeric `@value` entries. Arrays of values remain as arrays of individual `@value` objects. The `@type` field on a node is always an array of full IRI strings. Output must end with a newline character. Example key ordering within each value object: `@type` before `@value` for typed literals, `@language` before `@value` for language-tagged literals, `@id` alongside other properties for nodes.

trigger_keywords: how should compaction render the context
where does @context appear in compact output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Compacted JSON-LD output must be a single pretty-printed JSON object (not an array). The `@context` from the supplied context document must be appended as the last key in the output object. IRIs that match context terms must be shortened to their term alias. A single-element `@type` array must be compacted to a plain string. Multi-element `@type` arrays remain as arrays of compacted strings. Properties with `@type: @id` in the context cause their values to be rendered as compact IRI strings rather than node objects. Single-value property arrays are unwrapped to scalars where the context permits. Multi-value arrays remain as JSON arrays. Native scalar values (numbers) are preserved without `@value` wrapping when no type coercion conflict exists.

Flattening must first expand the input document (applying all context aliases and IRI resolution), then collect all graph nodes into a single top-level JSON array. The result is structurally identical to expansion for simple single-node documents. Native boolean and integer values are preserved as `{"@value": true}` and `{"@value": 123}` respectively without any type annotation when the original value has no explicit datatype. Prefixed terms like `e:bool` must be resolved to their full IRI (e.g., `http://example.org/vocab#bool`) using the context before appearing as keys.

Framed output must be a JSON object with a `@context` (from the frame's context) and a `@graph` array containing the matched top-level nodes. Nodes matched by the frame are embedded with their related nodes nested inside them according to the frame's shape. The `@context` is the frame's context compacted form. Linked nodes that appear as property values are embedded as full objects (not just `@id` references) when the frame specifies a sub-frame for that property. Type values are compacted using the frame context. The output ends with a newline. Single-value arrays for `@type` and single-valued object properties are unwrapped to plain strings/objects in the compacted framed output.

The `to_rdf` algorithm must output RDF statements in N-Quads format, one statement per line, each ending with ` .
`. IRI terms are wrapped in angle brackets `<...>`. Plain string literals are double-quoted. Language-tagged literals use the `"value"@lang` syntax. Typed literals use `"value"^^<datatype-iri>` syntax. Blank nodes are rendered as `_:bN` identifiers. The full output ends with a newline. For a document with no explicit `@id`, the subject becomes a blank node `_:b0`. RDF type triples use the predicate `<http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type>`.

The `from_rdf` algorithm accepts N-Quads text via the `nquads` input field (not a `document` field). Output is expanded JSON-LD as a pretty-printed array. RDF type triples (`rdf:type`) become `@type` arrays in the node. IRI object terms become `{"@id": "..."}` objects. Plain literal objects become `{"@value": "..."}`. Language-tagged literals produce `{"@value": "...", "@language": "..."}`. XSD-typed literals produce `{"@value": "...", "@type": "<xsd-iri>"}` where the value is the raw lexical string (not a parsed native number). This includes xsd:boolean, xsd:integer, xsd:decimal, and xsd:double — all remain as string `@value` with explicit `@type`.

The `normalize` algorithm converts JSON-LD to a canonical N-Quads dataset where blank node identifiers are stable and deterministic (RDNA/URDNA2015 canonicalization). A document with only an `@id` and no properties produces an empty output (just a newline `
`). A blank-node subject with a single `rdf:type` triple produces one canonical N-Quads line with a `_:c14n0` blank node identifier. Output ends with a newline.

When `namespaces: true` and `options.useNamespaces: true` are set on a `to_rdf` command, the output must NOT be N-Quads triples but instead a list of namespace prefix declarations in the format `namespace.<prefix>=<iri>\n` for each prefix found in the document context. For the `roundtrip_namespaces` algorithm, the output includes namespace declarations in the same format plus `contains.<compactIRI>=true\n` lines for each entry in the `contains` array that can be expressed using the given namespaces. The prefixes chosen must be the longest-match prefix that correctly shortens the IRI.

Errors must be reported to stdout as `error=<error_code>\n` (a plain key=value line, not JSON, not an exception stack trace). The error code `keyword_redefinition` must be emitted when a JSON-LD context attempts to redefine a JSON-LD keyword such as `@type` as a term mapping. The error code `loading_remote_context_failed` must be emitted when the `@context` value is a non-dereferenceable IRI string (e.g., a `tag:` URI that cannot be fetched). These are the only two error codes required by the public test suite, but the system must be extensible for additional error categories.

All JSON output (expand, compact, flatten, frame) must use a specific pretty-print style: 2-space indentation, a space after `:` in key-value pairs, and a space after `,` in inline arrays. Inline arrays (like `[ 50, 51 ]` in compact output for multi-value properties where the context expects a single value) are rendered on one line with spaces inside brackets. Multi-element arrays where each element is an object are rendered with each object on its own indented block. The Jackson `SerializationFeature.INDENT_OUTPUT` style with `DefaultPrettyPrinter` or equivalent must match the exact spacing seen in test expected outputs.

The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin. The `algorithm` field selects the operation: `expand`, `compact`, `flatten`, `frame`, `to_rdf`, `from_rdf`, `normalize`, or `roundtrip_namespaces`. The `document` field carries the JSON-LD input for most operations. The `nquads` field (string) carries N-Quads text for `from_rdf`. The `context` field carries the compaction context for `compact`. The `frame` field carries the frame document for `frame`. The `base` field provides the base IRI. The `options` object carries processing options. The `namespaces` field (boolean or object) and `triples` array are used for namespace-related operations. The `contains` array is used for `roundtrip_namespaces` containment checks.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to how we handled prefix compaction in the graph client module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The namespace/prefix handling logic described in feature8_namespaces.json — specifically the `to_rdf` with `useNamespaces: true` option that outputs `namespace.<prefix>=<iri>` lines, and the `roundtrip_namespaces` algorithm that outputs both namespace declarations and `contains.<compactIRI>=true` lines

context_pointers.vague_reference: that RDF utility we prototyped for the semantic web client last year
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The full JsonLdProcessor API covering all 7 algorithms (expand, compact, flatten, frame, toRDF, fromRDF, normalize) as defined across feature1 through feature7 test cases, implemented against the JSON-LD 1.0 specification

context_pointers.vague_reference: report problems cleanly without exposing internal exception noise to callers
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error output format defined in feature9_errors.json: stdout line `error=<error_code>` using normalized category codes like `keyword_redefinition` and `loading_remote_context_failed`, suppressing Java exception details
The `normalize` algorithm using URDNA2015 blank node canonicalization, outputting sorted canonical N-Quads with deterministic `_:c14nN` blank node labels as shown in feature7_normalize.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the technical details on that specific point. You'd need to look at the existing test cases in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or check with the team member who worked on the semantic web client prototype — they'd have the context on how that behavior is supposed to work.

feature1_expand.json.description: Expand compact JSON-LD documents into full IRI-based node objects while applying context aliases, type coercion, and default language rules.

feature2_compact.json.description: Compact expanded JSON-LD using a supplied context so IRIs, types, native values, and context declarations are rendered in their shortest valid JSON-LD form.

feature3_flatten.json.description: Flatten JSON-LD into a deterministic node list that preserves graph membership, native values, and embedded graph data after expansion.

feature4_frame.json.description: Frame JSON-LD by selecting graph nodes that match a frame and embedding or referencing related nodes according to the frame and processing options.

feature5_to_rdf.json.description: Convert JSON-LD documents into RDF statements, including literal language tags, RDF type triples, RDF lists, typed literals, and decimal lexical values.

http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#lab.@value: A plain literal with a lang tag.

cases.expected_output: _:b0 <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#label> "A plain literal with a lang tag."@en-us .

feature6_from_rdf.json.description: Convert N-Quads RDF input into expanded JSON-LD while reconstructing lists, blank-node structures, named graphs, native values, and RDF type handling.

feature7_normalize.json.description: Normalize JSON-LD into canonical N-Quads so blank node identifiers and statement ordering are stable for equivalent RDF datasets.

feature8_namespaces.json.description: Preserve valid namespace prefixes during RDF conversion and choose compact names that shorten IRIs without losing prefix mappings.

feature9_errors.json.description: Report invalid JSON-LD contexts and invalid IRI values as normalized language-neutral error categories instead of runtime exception details.
